_provenance: Claude-authored original Kaoyan (English I) style reading, batch F4. ~300-350 word abstract argumentative passages, 4 MCQs each (attitude/inference/main idea/argument/vocabulary). Topics distinct from F1-F3. DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage: A society holds together not only by laws and force but, more fundamentally, by trust — by the countless unspoken assumptions that allow strangers to cooperate, that let us accept the word of a doctor, the report of a newspaper, the verdict of a court. This trust is so much the invisible medium of social life that we notice it, like the air, chiefly when it begins to fail. And there are signs, in many places, that it is failing. The institutions that once commanded a broad, if imperfect, confidence — the professions, the press, the organs of government and science — increasingly meet a public disposed to suspect their competence and impugn their motives. The causes are several, and not all are discreditable; institutions have at times betrayed the trust placed in them, and a measure of scepticism is the proper response of a thinking citizen. But the erosion has gone beyond healthy scepticism into something more corrosive: a generalised distrust that no evidence can correct, because it has ceased to be a response to evidence at all. Where this condition takes hold, a peculiar paralysis follows. If no authority can be trusted, then every question becomes a matter of private opinion, and the shared factual ground on which collective decision depends simply dissolves. Citizens retreat into rival realities, each with its own trusted sources and its own dismissed ones, and the very possibility of reasoning together is lost. The danger is not that people will believe too little, but that, having abandoned the disciplined trust that knowledge requires, they will believe anything — for the mind that trusts nothing established proves, paradoxically, defenceless against whatever flatters its preferences. To rebuild a warranted trust, neither credulous nor cynical, may be among the more urgent and least tractable tasks a fractured society can set itself. The rebuilding of trust cannot be achieved by exhortation alone, nor by the demand that citizens be less suspicious; it requires institutions that earn confidence by their conduct and citizens willing to extend a provisional, revisable trust, neither the blind faith that invites betrayal nor the reflexive cynicism that makes cooperation impossible.

questions.prompt: How does the writer describe trust in society?
options: As the invisible medium of social life, noticed chiefly when it fails.
As enforced solely by law and force.

questions.prompt: How does the writer distinguish healthy scepticism from the current condition?
options: The current condition is a generalised distrust that no evidence can correct.

questions.prompt: What 'paralysis' follows when no authority can be trusted?
options: Every question becomes private opinion and shared factual ground dissolves.

questions.prompt: What paradox does the writer note about the mind that trusts nothing?
options: It proves defenceless against whatever flatters its preferences.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 信任是社会生活无形之媒、失灵时才察觉)、细节(B 当前是无证据可纠正的泛化不信任、异于健康怀疑)、细节(B 谁都不可信则一切沦为私见、共同事实基础瓦解)、主旨/推断(B 什么都不信的心智反而对迎合其偏好之物毫无防御)。

sets.passage: There is an old quarrel, never quite settled, between those who hold that the things we make should be judged solely by their usefulness and those who insist that beauty, too, has its claims. The utilitarian view has the appeal of hard-headedness: an object exists to perform a function, and ornament that adds nothing to its working is mere waste, a frivolous expense that a serious age should disdain. On this account, the well-designed thing is simply the one that does its job most efficiently, and to ask of it anything further is sentimentality. Yet this austere doctrine, pressed consistently, runs against something deep in human nature. We are not, it turns out, content to be served by the merely functional; we respond to the form of the things around us, are cheered or oppressed by them, and find in a beautiful object a satisfaction that its bare utility cannot explain. Nor is this response a trivial weakness to be disciplined away. The objects among which we pass our lives shape, by their presence, the texture of our days, and a world built to the standard of efficiency alone — however well it works — would be a world impoverished in a dimension that matters to creatures such as we are. The deeper truth may be that the opposition between beauty and use is partly false. The most enduring artefacts are often those in which the two are reconciled, in which the form that pleases is also the form that serves, and the pleasure we take in them is inseparable from our sense of their fitness. To build well is not to choose between the useful and the beautiful but to achieve, in the same act, a thing that works and a thing that delights — and the civilisations we most admire are precisely those that refused to treat the second aim as a luxury they could not afford. To insist on beauty in the things we make is not to indulge a frivolous taste at the expense of function but to honour the whole of our nature; the societies remembered with admiration are not those that built most cheaply, but those whose works still give pleasure long after their practical occasion is forgotten.

questions.prompt: What does the utilitarian view of design hold?
options: That objects should be judged solely by usefulness, and ornament is waste.

questions.prompt: Why does the austere doctrine 'run against something deep in human nature'?
options: Because we respond to the form of things and find satisfaction beyond bare utility.
Because objects have no effect on us.

questions.prompt: What 'deeper truth' does the writer propose?
options: Beauty and use are wholly opposed.
The opposition between beauty and use is partly false; the best artefacts reconcile them.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say the admired civilisations refused to do?
options: To treat beauty as a luxury they could not afford.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 功利论:只以用途判物、装饰即浪费)、细节(B 人对形式有回应、所得满足超出纯用途)、细节(B 更深真理:美与用之对立部分为假、佳物兼之)、主旨(B 被敬仰的文明拒绝把美当成负担不起的奢侈)。

sets.passage: What we ask of work has changed in ways we have scarcely paused to examine. For most of history, labour was understood as a necessity and a burden, the price exacted by survival, valued for its fruits and not for itself; no one expected the toil of the field or the workshop to confer meaning or to express the self. The modern world has quietly raised the stakes. We now demand of work not merely a wage but fulfilment — that it engage our talents, accord with our values, and supply a sense of purpose that earlier ages sought elsewhere. This expectation is in some ways a noble one, the fruit of a conviction that human beings deserve more than drudgery. But it is also a source of widespread and peculiarly modern unhappiness. For the great majority of jobs, in any economy, are not and cannot be vehicles of self-realisation; they are necessary tasks that must be done, useful to others and adequately rewarded, but offering little scope for the soul's expression. To insist that such work ought to be fulfilling is to guarantee disappointment, and to burden the ordinary worker with a sense of failure for not having found in his job a meaning it was never structured to provide. There is a question, too, whether the demand is even coherent — whether a society can function if everyone insists on labour that is personally fulfilling, when so much necessary work is, by its nature, none too inspiring. The older view, for all its grimness, contained a wisdom we have lost: that the meaning of a life need not be sought in one's work at all, that a job may be honourably done and decently paid without being asked to bear a weight of significance it cannot carry, and that the self may find its fullest expression in the hours and the relations that labour exists precisely to make possible. To recover this older wisdom is not to counsel resignation, nor to deny that some find deep meaning in their work; it is to relieve the many whose labour cannot bear such weight of a burden never theirs to carry, and to remind a distracted age that a life's significance is measured by far more than the work that fills its days.

questions.prompt: How was labour understood 'for most of history'?
options: As a necessity and burden, valued for its fruits, not itself.
As the chief source of meaning.
As an expression of the self.

questions.prompt: What does the modern world now demand of work?
options: Fulfilment — engagement of talents, accord with values, a sense of purpose.

questions.prompt: Why is this modern demand 'a source of unhappiness'?
options: Because most jobs cannot be vehicles of self-realisation, guaranteeing disappointment.

questions.prompt: What 'wisdom' does the writer find in the older view?
options: That work must always fulfil us.
That meaning need not be sought in work, which can be honourably done without bearing such weight.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 历史上劳动是必需与负担、为其果实而非自身被重视)、细节(B 现代要求工作给予成就感与意义)、细节(B 多数工作无法自我实现、此要求注定失望)、主旨/态度(B 旧观点之智慧:意义不必在工作中寻、工作可体面完成而不必承此重负)。

sets.passage: Tolerance is rightly counted among the cardinal virtues of an open society, the disposition that allows people of differing convictions to live together in peace. Yet tolerance, examined, conceals a paradox that has troubled reflective minds and that no society can entirely escape. The paradox is this: if a society is tolerant without limit, extending its tolerance even to those who would destroy tolerance itself, then the tolerant will in time be overcome by the intolerant, and tolerance with them. Unlimited tolerance, in other words, is self-defeating; to survive, a tolerant order must be prepared, at some point, to be intolerant of the forces that would abolish it. But this conclusion, once admitted, opens a dangerous door. For every society contains those eager to brand their opponents as enemies of tolerance, and so to justify, in tolerance's own name, the suppression of views they merely dislike. The principle that the intolerant need not be tolerated can become a license for a new intolerance, dressed in the robes of the virtue it betrays. There is no formula that resolves this difficulty, no rule that marks precisely where legitimate self-defence ends and illegitimate suppression begins. The line must be drawn by judgement, case by case, and drawn always with a wary awareness of how easily the defence of openness can become its opposite. The wisest course holds two truths in an uncomfortable tension: that a free society may not, on pain of suicide, tolerate everything; and that it must extend its intolerance only to the genuinely intolerable, with the greatest reluctance, and never as a convenient weapon against the merely disagreeable. To forget the first truth is to be naive; to forget the second is to become the very thing one claims to oppose. To navigate this paradox demands not a rule but a virtue: the steady judgement that distinguishes genuine threats to an open order from mere disagreement, and the self-restraint to act against the former while protecting the latter; a society that loses this judgement will fall either to an enemy it was too tolerant to resist or to the very tyranny it set out to oppose.

questions.prompt: What is the 'paradox of tolerance' as the writer states it?
options: That unlimited tolerance, extended even to the intolerant, is self-defeating.

questions.prompt: What 'dangerous door' does the conclusion open?
options: People may brand opponents 'intolerant' to justify suppressing views they dislike.

questions.prompt: How must the line be drawn, according to the writer?
options: By judgement, case by case, wary of how defence becomes its opposite.

questions.prompt: What two truths must the wisest course hold in tension?
options: That tolerance is worthless and intolerance is good.
That a free society cannot tolerate everything, yet must restrict only the genuinely intolerable.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 悖论:无限宽容连不宽容者也容、终将自毁)、细节(B 危险之门:借'反不宽容'之名打压不喜之见)、细节(B 界线须凭逐案判断、警惕防御变其反面)、主旨(B 既不能容一切、又只对真正不可容者最不情愿地设限)。

sets.passage: Does the language we speak shape the way we think? The question has a long and contentious history, and the answers proposed have swung between extremes that the evidence supports neither. At one pole stands the strong claim that language determines thought — that the categories of our native tongue form a prison from which the mind cannot escape, so that speakers of different languages inhabit, in effect, different worlds, unable fully to conceive what their grammar does not provide for. This bold thesis, influential for a time, has not survived examination. Translation, however imperfect, is possible; speakers readily grasp concepts their language lacks a single word for; and the supposed cognitive prisons turn out, on inspection, to have doors. The strong claim, in short, is false. But the opposite extreme — that language is a mere transparent vehicle, exerting no influence whatever on thought — has fared little better against the evidence. Careful experiment has shown that the particular features of a language do leave their mark on the habitual attention and judgement of its speakers: the distinctions a tongue obliges one to draw, the categories it makes salient, incline the mind, subtly but measurably, in characteristic directions. Language does not imprison thought, but it does nudge it; it furnishes not a cage but a set of well-worn paths that one may leave but tends, without effort, to follow. The truth thus lies, as so often, between the dramatic alternatives that first present themselves. Our languages neither determine what we can think nor leave our thinking untouched, but shape its habitual tendencies in ways we are mostly unaware of — an influence real enough to matter, yet far from the absolute dominion that the bolder theorists once proclaimed. To hold this middle position is harder than embracing either extreme, for it offers neither the drama of determinism nor the comforting simplicity of supposing language a mere window; but it fits the evidence, leaving us with a picture at once more modest and more interesting, of minds genuinely shaped, but never finally shackled, by the words they happen to have inherited.

questions.prompt: What is the 'strong claim' about language and thought?
options: That language has no effect on thought.
That language determines thought, forming a prison the mind cannot escape.

questions.prompt: Why has the strong claim 'not survived examination'?
options: Because translation is possible and speakers grasp concepts their language lacks a word for.

questions.prompt: What has careful experiment shown about language's influence?
options: Language's features subtly but measurably incline speakers' habitual attention and judgement.

questions.prompt: Where does the truth lie, according to the writer?
options: Between the extremes: language nudges thought without determining or leaving it untouched.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 强主张:语言决定思想、是无法逃脱的牢笼)、细节(B 翻译可行、人能掌握母语无单词之概念、故强主张不成立)、细节(B 实验表明语言特征可测地影响习惯性注意与判断)、主旨(B 真理在两极之间:语言轻推而非决定思想)。

sets.passage: A persistent assumption of much modern thinking about education is that the path to learning should be made as smooth and as pleasant as possible — that difficulty is an obstacle to be removed, friction a defect to be engineered away, and the ideal lesson one that slips into the mind without effort or discomfort. The assumption is humane in intent and not wholly mistaken; needless obstacles help no one, and a learner crushed by difficulty learns nothing. But pressed too far, the doctrine of ease betrays a misunderstanding of how the mind actually acquires and retains what it knows. For a growing body of evidence suggests that certain kinds of difficulty, far from impeding learning, are essential to it. The effort of struggling to recall a fact, of wrestling with a problem before being shown its solution, of encountering material in a form that resists easy absorption — these 'desirable difficulties', as they have been called, produce learning that is deeper and more durable than the frictionless transmission they replace. What is gained without effort, it seems, is held without firmness; the mind values, and retains, what it has had to work for. The implication runs counter to a powerful intuition. We naturally suppose that the lesson which feels easy is the one that teaches best, and we judge our learning by the fluency of the moment. But this feeling is a poor guide, and may be precisely inverted: the very ease that gratifies in the moment often signals shallow and fleeting acquisition, while the struggle that frustrates may be laying down the durable understanding that lasts. To learn well, then, is not to avoid difficulty but to embrace the right kinds of it — a conclusion that overturns the comfortable assumption on which so much well-meaning pedagogy has been built. To act on this insight requires a certain courage, from teachers and learners alike, for it means deliberately tolerating a discomfort every instinct urges us to remove; yet the reward is a learning that endures, and a pedagogy brave enough to let its students struggle may serve them far better than one that, in its kindness, smooths every difficulty from their path.

questions.prompt: What 'assumption' about education does the writer examine?
options: That learning should be made difficult.
That the path to learning should be made as smooth and effortless as possible.

questions.prompt: What does the evidence suggest about certain difficulties?
options: 'Desirable difficulties' produce deeper and more durable learning.

questions.prompt: Why is the 'feeling' that an easy lesson teaches best 'a poor guide'?
options: Because ease always signals deep learning.
Because ease often signals shallow, fleeting acquisition while struggle builds durable understanding.

questions.prompt: What conclusion does the writer reach about learning well?
options: To embrace the right kinds of difficulty rather than avoid them.
To judge learning by momentary fluency.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 假设:学习之路应尽量顺滑无费力)、细节(B '可欲之难'产生更深更持久的学习)、细节(B 当下之顺常意味浅而易逝、挣扎反建持久理解、故那种感觉是坏向导)、主旨(B 学好不是避难、而是拥抱恰当之难)。

sets.passage: There was a time, not long past, when a decent reticence about one's inner life was counted a mark of dignity, and the exposure of private feeling to public view a breach of taste. That world has largely vanished. We live now in a culture of confession, in which the disclosure of the most intimate experiences — once reserved for the closest intimates, or the priest, or no one — has become a public performance, solicited by the media and rewarded with attention. The change is often celebrated as a liberation, a casting-off of repressive reticence in favour of an honest openness, and there is something in the claim; secrecy can be a prison, and the shame that once silenced suffering did real harm. But the confessional culture has costs that its champions overlook. The first is that the relentless exhibition of the inner life may, paradoxically, impoverish it. A feeling immediately broadcast is a feeling not yet understood; the rush to disclose forecloses the private reflection through which experience is digested and made one's own, and substitutes the cheap reward of an audience for the harder work of self-knowledge. The second cost is the erosion of intimacy itself. The value of a confidence lies partly in its being reserved, in the trust that selects this hearer and not the world; what is told to everyone is told, in the deepest sense, to no one, and a culture that confesses indiscriminately may find that it has lost the very capacity for the intimate exchange it imagines itself to be extending. To value privacy is not, then, to cling to a repressive past. It is to recognise that some things are deepened by being kept, that the inner life requires a sheltered space in which to grow, and that the self ceaselessly exhibited is a self that may never quite be possessed. To defend a measure of reticence is not to recommend the old shame that silenced suffering, but to recover a truth the confessional age has mislaid: that not everything is improved by being shared, and that the self, like a seed, may need a covering of darkness in which to germinate before it is exposed to the light.

questions.prompt: How is the 'culture of confession' often celebrated?
options: As a liberation, a casting-off of repressive reticence for honest openness.

questions.prompt: What is the 'first cost' the writer identifies?
options: That immediate broadcast of feeling forecloses the reflection through which experience is understood.

questions.prompt: Why does confessing indiscriminately erode intimacy?
options: What is told to everyone is, in the deepest sense, told to no one.

questions.prompt: What does valuing privacy mean, according to the writer?
options: Recognising that some things are deepened by being kept, and the inner life needs sheltered space.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 坦白文化被誉为对压抑缄默的解放)、细节(B 第一代价:即时广播情感、扼杀消化经验的反思)、细节(B 告知所有人=深层意义上告知无人、侵蚀亲密)、主旨(B 珍视隐私是认得有些事因被保留而加深、内心需庇护空间)。

sets.passage: Every age has its characteristic idolatry, and ours, beyond serious dispute, is the worship of youth. The young are held up as the standard of all that is desirable — of beauty, vitality, relevance — and the whole apparatus of commerce and culture conspires to present age as a misfortune to be concealed, delayed, and where possible reversed. So pervasive is this attitude that it passes for nature, and we forget that other ages and other cultures have seen the matter very differently, honouring in the old a wisdom and authority that compensated, and more than compensated, for the loss of youth's advantages. The consequences of the modern view are not trivial. To define a human life by the standard of youth is to condemn the greater part of it to a long decline, to teach people to dread the very process of their own maturing, and to rob age of the dignity that might make it bearable, even welcome. It encourages the old to ape the young in a futile and undignified pursuit, and it deprives the young themselves of the models of a graceful and meaningful later life that they will one day need. There is, moreover, a falsehood at the heart of the cult. The goods of youth are real but partial; they are purchased, characteristically, at the cost of the understanding, the perspective and the settled judgement that only time can bring. A civilisation that can see in age nothing but the subtraction of youthful goods has blinded itself to the distinctive goods of age — has mistaken one season of life for the whole, and so impoverished its understanding of what a complete human existence might be. To recover a juster view would be to see the course of a life not as a brief ascent followed by a long fall, but as a sequence of seasons, each with its own losses and its own irreplaceable gifts. To recover such a view would benefit not only the old but the young, who are taught by the cult of youth to dread their own futures; a culture that honoured each season of existence for its own gifts would free its members from a long apprenticeship in self-contempt, and restore to age the dignity that makes the passage of the years bearable, and even welcome.

questions.prompt: What does the writer call the 'characteristic idolatry' of our age?
How have other ages viewed age differently?
options: Honouring in the old a wisdom and authority that compensated for youth's loss.

questions.prompt: What 'falsehood' lies at the heart of the cult of youth?
options: That the goods of youth are purchased at the cost of understanding and judgement only time brings.

questions.prompt: What 'juster view' does the writer recommend?
options: Seeing life as a brief ascent and a long fall.
Seeing life as a sequence of seasons, each with its own losses and gifts.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 本时代的偶像崇拜是崇拜青春)、细节(B 别的时代敬重老者之智慧与权威以补偿青春之失)、细节(B 谎言:青春之善以只有时间能给的理解判断为代价)、主旨(B 更公允之见:把人生看作各有得失与馈赠的季节序列)。

sets.passage: Justice and mercy are both reckoned virtues, and we praise them almost in the same breath, as though they were natural allies. Yet between them lies a tension that careful reflection cannot dissolve, for in their pure forms they pull in opposite directions, and to honour the one is often to slight the other. Justice, strictly understood, is the giving to each of precisely what he is due — no more in reward, no less in punishment — and its emblem is the scale, impartial and exact. Mercy, by contrast, is the disposition to give less than is due in punishment, to temper the deserved penalty with compassion for the offender. The conflict is plain: to be merciful is, by definition, to depart from the strict requirements of justice, to let the wrongdoer off more lightly than his desert demands. A judge who is perfectly just cannot also be merciful, for mercy is precisely the remission of a portion of deserved punishment, and to remit what justice requires is, in justice's own terms, a wrong. How, then, can both be virtues? The resolution, if there is one, lies in recognising that justice, for all its dignity, is not the only or the highest consideration in human affairs, and that a world ruled by justice alone, unsoftened by mercy, would be a cold and even a cruel place. The strict enforcement of every desert, the exaction of every deserved penalty without remission, describes not a perfect society but a pitiless one. Mercy acknowledges what justice, in its rigour, cannot: that the offender is a person and not merely a case, that circumstances extenuate, that the capacity to forgive is among the things that make us human. To hold justice and mercy together, neither collapsing one into the other, is not to resolve their tension but to live within it — accepting that the fullest humanity requires both a respect for desert and a willingness, on occasion, to set it aside.

questions.prompt: How does the writer define justice 'strictly understood'?
options: Giving to each precisely what he is due, no more and no less.

questions.prompt: Why can a 'perfectly just' judge not also be merciful?
options: Because mercy and justice are identical.
Because mercy remits a portion of deserved punishment, which justice counts a wrong.

questions.prompt: What would a world ruled by justice alone be like?
options: A cold, pitiless and even cruel place.

questions.prompt: What does mercy acknowledge that justice 'cannot'?
options: That desert is all that matters.
That the offender is a person, that circumstances extenuate, and that forgiveness makes us human.
That punishment should always be exacted.
That justice is the highest good.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 正义=各得其应得、不多不少)、细节(B 仁慈减免应得之罚、按正义自身即为错、故完美的正义法官不能同时仁慈)、细节(B 唯正义之世界冷酷无情)、主旨(B 仁慈承认罪者是人非案、情有可原、宽恕使人为人)。

sets.passage: The ideal of objective journalism — of a reporting that simply mirrors events, untouched by the views of the reporter — is among the proudest claims of the modern press, and among the most misleading. It is misleading not because journalists are uniquely dishonest, but because the ideal misdescribes what reporting, by its nature, must do. For the world does not present itself to the journalist as a finished story, neatly bounded and arranged. It offers an inexhaustible chaos of happenings, from which the reporter must select the few that will be told, and selection is never neutral. To decide that this event is news and that one is not, to choose which facts are relevant and which may be omitted, to determine what background a reader needs and what may be assumed — these are judgements, inescapably, and judgements are shaped by the values, assumptions and interests of the one who makes them. The very framing of a story, the questions it treats as open and those it treats as settled, embodies a point of view, however invisible to the reporter himself. This does not mean that all journalism is equally biased, or that the distinction between honest and dishonest reporting collapses; that would be a lazy cynicism as false as the naive faith it replaces. There remains every difference between the reporter who strives, against the grain of his own preferences, to be fair, to represent opposing views, to follow the evidence where it leads — and the propagandist who bends the facts to a predetermined end. But the honest reporter's achievement is not objectivity, which is impossible, but fairness, which is hard; not the absence of a viewpoint, but the disciplined effort to prevent one's viewpoint from distorting the truth. To understand this is to read the news more wisely — neither as a transparent window on reality nor as mere manipulation, but as the inevitably partial work of fallible human judgement, to be weighed accordingly.

questions.prompt: Why is the ideal of objective journalism 'misleading'?
options: Because reporting must select from a chaos of events, and selection is never neutral.
Because the world presents finished stories.

questions.prompt: What does 'the very framing of a story' embody?
options: A point of view, however invisible to the reporter.

questions.prompt: What distinction does the writer refuse to let collapse?
options: Between the reporter who strives to be fair and the propagandist who bends facts.

questions.prompt: What is the honest reporter's real achievement, according to the writer?
options: Fairness — the disciplined effort to prevent one's viewpoint from distorting the truth.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 客观之理想误导:报道须从混沌事件中选择、选择从不中立)、细节(B 故事的框架本身体现一种立场)、细节(B 不可让'力求公正之记者'与'歪曲事实之宣传者'之别崩塌)、主旨(B 诚实记者的成就不是客观而是公正——防止己见歪曲真相)。
